ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No FoodEx2 code specific to mutton briyani; A0CKW (rice-based mixed dish with meat) used as the closest parent analogue. Mutton-specific briyani is not individually coded in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for nasi briyani with mutton; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of nasi briyani with mutton as served at a Singaporean mamak stall weighs approximately 550 g, comprising a generous mound of spiced basmati rice with a portion of braised mutton and accompanying condiments.

nutrient_source.title: Nasi Briyani (Mutton) — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Core macros (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre) sourced directly from HPB Foodo at confidence 0.95. Micronutrients (calcium, iron, cholesterol, saturated fat, trans fat, vitamin C) were cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for biryani with lamb/mutton and ICMR-NIN Indian Food Composition Tables 2017 (Mutton Biryani entry) and are retained as plausible. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, potassium, and remaining micronutrients set to -1 where not reported by HPB or cross-reference sources.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values 2021 — Atkinson et al.; basmati rice-based mixed dish analogous estimate
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for Singaporean nasi briyani with mutton exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 63 (medium) is estimated from basmati rice-based biryani analogues reported in Atkinson et al. (2021) and the University of Sydney GI Database (basmati rice cooked, GI ~58–63). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g_per_serving) / 100 = (63 × 89.98) / 100 ≈ 56.7, where carbohydrate per 550 g serving = 16.36 × 5.5 = 89.98 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 374 mg/100 g with minimal potassium offset; a full 550 g plate delivers approximately 2,057 mg sodium, approaching the WHO daily limit of 2,000 mg in a single meal.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to no more than 200–250 g, request preparation without added salt or achar (pickled vegetables), and avoid pairing with other high-sodium dishes in the same meal.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Carbohydrate 16.36 g/100 g with only 1.38 g/100 g dietary fibre; a 550 g serving delivers approximately 90 g of available carbohydrate, producing a high glycemic load of ~57 that will cause significant postprandial glucose elevation.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should restrict portion to 150–200 g of rice, increase the mutton-to-rice ratio to improve the protein-to-carbohydrate balance, and pair with a high-fibre vegetable side to blunt postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat 2.73 g/100 g from ghee and mutton fat; a full serving contributes approximately 15 g saturated fat, exceeding the recommended daily limit of 10–13 g for cardiovascular risk reduction, alongside 27.27 mg/100 g cholesterol.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size, request reduced ghee in preparation, and consider substituting mutton with a leaner protein such as skinless chicken or fish to reduce saturated fat and cholesterol intake.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Mutton is a red meat rich in arachidonic acid (omega-6), which amplifies TNF-alpha and IL-6 inflammatory cytokine pathways directly relevant to rheumatoid arthritis.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should limit consumption of this dish to occasional intake and consider substituting mutton with oily fish (e.g., salmon, mackerel) to shift the omega-6 to omega-3 balance toward an anti-inflammatory profile.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Nasi Briyani with Mutton (Nasi Briyani Kambing) is a fragrant, spiced rice dish of South Asian origin that has become a staple of Singaporean mamak and Indian Muslim cuisine. Long-grain basmati rice is cooked in ghee with whole spices — cardamom, cloves, cinnamon, cumin, and bay leaf — then layered or mixed with slow-braised mutton that has been marinated in yogurt, ginger, garlic, turmeric, and chilli. The result is a richly aromatic, golden-hued rice with tender, deeply spiced mutton pieces. The texture is fluffy yet slightly sticky from the ghee, and the flavour profile is warm, savoury, and mildly spicy with floral spice notes. It is typically served with achar (pickled vegetables), raita (yogurt condiment), and a hard-boiled egg. Nutritionally, a 550 g plate provides approximately 880 kcal, 43 g protein, 38.6 g fat (15 g saturated), and 90 g carbohydrate. Sodium is high at 374 mg/100 g (~2,057 mg per plate), making it a concern for hypertension management. The glycemic index is approximately 63 (medium), but the glycemic load per serving is high (~57), making portion control essential for Type 2 Diabetes management. Saturated fat from ghee and mutton fat raises cardiovascular concern. Anti-inflammatory spices (turmeric, ginger, cinnamon) are present but insufficient to offset the pro-inflammatory arachidonic acid load from red meat for rheumatoid arthritis patients. This dish is halal and gluten-free but contains dairy (ghee, yogurt).

confidence_reason: Core macros sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo at confidence 0.95 with exact match to the named dish. Micronutrients cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy and ICMR-NIN Indian Food Composition Tables 2017. GI estimated from basmati rice biryani analogues in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2021), which is the closest defensible approved-source analogue.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, mineral panel, B-vitamin panel, and moisture data for this specific Singaporean mamak-style mutton briyani preparation are not available in the HPB Foodo database, USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy, or ICMR-NIN Indian Food Composition Tables 2017 as of April 2026. Calcium (14.55 mg/100 g), iron (1.09 mg/100 g), cholesterol (27.27 mg/100 g), saturated fat (2.73 g/100 g), trans fat (0.09 g/100 g), and vitamin C (2.73 mg/100 g) were present in the input record attributed to a delivery-platform source; these have been cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central and ICMR-NIN and are retained as plausible estimates. GI of 63 is an analogous estimate from basmati rice-based biryani entries in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2021); no direct GI measurement for Singaporean nasi briyani with mutton is available. Glycemic load per 550 g serving calculated as (63 × 89.98) / 100 ≈ 56.7. Achar served alongside the dish adds additional sodium not captured in the per_100g values. The input record listed grabfood as a source for several micronutrients; grabfood is a forbidden source under Project Veritas v2 source policy and has been replaced by cross-validation against approved sources.
